# Extracao: transcription_mp4_02-4_Funnel_System_Setup

---

This is Deepesh and I'm going to take you through the setup of the full funnel system. So in the previous video, assuming you've seen them sequentially, you'd have seen the kind of typical account structure and funnel that I like to use with the four different levels for cold, warm, hot and existing, where the cold audience is, you know, they haven't seen it before, warm is engaged, hot is website visits and existing is existing customers. How do I structure that within an account? What I like to do is first of all use the right naming convention so I know which part of the funnel that I'm looking at when I'm analyzing my campaigns. So for the cold audience targeting, here I put the level as well as the part of the funnel. You can see further down, we've got level two, three, four existing in the different naming conventions. I also like to include the objective at this area as well, because it just helps me to know which objective I'm looking at when I'm analyzing performance and looking across, because there'll be different performances that come through based on different parts of the funnel. Here, I will generally either put the type of product, whether it's e-commerce or lead gen, et cetera, or I might just put the product itself or the category. This is just a naming differentiator so that I can later on look at my analysis in Ants Manager and pull the data by name. So I might want to say pull me the data where campaign name equals cold and product equals X, for example. And then I just like to put a country at the end as well. I like to generally split my campaigns by country, especially if they're larger. In some cases, I'll just do a general worldwide campaign. That's entirely up to you. But the key thing here is just noticing how I'm setting up my campaign structure based on the part of the funnel that I'm playing with as well. Then going down into warm, again, it's just looking at the different types of objectives here. With the warm audience, the challenge is that your audience size is, depending on the spend, are going to be that much smaller. If your audience sizes are in the small hundreds, then just merge it within your cold campaign. So include your fans, et cetera, et cetera. It's only worth really splitting out your engaged audience if you have different creatives for them to play with. And if you've got a sizable audience. So for example, if you're running a video views engagement campaign and you've got 10, 50 people in there, it's probably not going to be a great use of your budget to target those amount of people. If you've got hundreds and into the thousands into your retargeting for video engagement, then this is great. And this is a great setup as well. One thing I will reference is these are my typical setups for objectives. For you, it depends on your product, your creative and what you're using. When you're looking at the graduation framework, which you'll come to, my preference is always to use the page post engagement where possible. There are some situations where I don't, such as if I'm doing app install or lead gen, I might do my graduation framework in those objectives. But generally, I'm always running a page post engagement for testing and building up social proof as well. Video views, the objective is fairly similar to the page post engagement, but you get better preference for running videos there. And then website conversion, which would be my main growth and money making campaign here. Now going into the smaller audiences, you've got a couple of choices. If you've got smaller audiences, you're not going to optimize so well for purchases, for example. So there's two ways of running this. If your audience size is small into hundreds or the thousands, a traffic campaign can be a good way of just bringing people back into your site, especially if they've already engaged. So I would never run a traffic campaign on a cold audience because it just bring back really bad quality traffic. If they're warmed up, I might consider it. However, more than likely, I would run a website conversion campaign optimized for landing page views that is far more efficient for your spend than using the traffic objective. With a traffic objective, what Facebook is doing is just literally throwing people off of Facebook to your site. It doesn't care if they even get there or not. And that's probably not an ideal outcome for you. Whereas with website conversion, Facebook is handholding them from Facebook to your site and making sure they land on your page. And that is far more efficient for your growth as well. Then going into the hot audiences, typically I would set up a website conversion campaign and maybe test against the traffic as well. Now, the reason why I might consider it a traffic campaign for my hot retargeting campaign is because at this stage, if they've hit your cold ads potentially being warmed up through engagement ads and visited your site, and let's say they are ad to cart abandonment potential customers as well, because they are so super hot, I just want them back in the site. So with a traffic campaign, you're saying to Facebook, I don't care, just send them all back to me. I want them back. And you're making the algorithm work less harder to find those right people as well. So especially for cold audiences, it can work. However, the alternative is to again use website conversion and maybe test running impressions, daily unique rich or landing page views. But really, if you're running, whether it's an info product, digital or e-commerce, when you're running a website conversion objective on an audience, that's really where you're bringing back a lot of people through your funnel and converting a lot more people as well. So you should be hitting Facebook's ideal number of 50 conversions a week. If you're not, then you're going to have to play around with the objectives and try and get more people to come through your funnel as well. And then finally into the bottom part of funnel existing customers. Again it's the same situation. You're probably going to be limited by numbers. Again it depends on how much volume you're pushing into your spend. And the preference will be website conversion to find people are likely to buy again. But again, play around with the objectives here as well. You can even do a video views campaign just to try and increase the reach, especially knowing that this is a prime audience. The final part I just wanted to take you through is just to give you an example of a live view of how I use this setup is this is an e-com client where we've been spending on testing, we've been developing video view campaigns, we've got our money maker doing website conversion, we've tested a traffic campaign, and we're running predominantly, sorry for our warm campaign we did a traffic test, but predominantly we're running our retargeting. And you can see here we've done a fairly even split of spend on the traffic and website conversion objectives for this client. And it's been fluctuations, but on average the website conversion has performed slightly better. But you know, I get a lot of questions about how much should I put into testing into retargeting into cold, etc. When this account started, the majority of spend was in the testing. And actually, although the test spend has gone up over time, it's remained fairly static once we hit the point of growth. So that 1400, this is I think a 30 day period. So that 1400 has remained about there or thereabouts, whereas this kind of spend on cold and retargeting has really kind of picked up in that time. So as you're starting to increase your prospecting budget and increase your retargeting, naturally the percentage going into testing will drop. But really it's about making sure you've got enough testing going on, you're running the graduation strategy, you're running your creatives to test what to push into your main campaigns. And just making sure you are always testing. And you know, I play with budgets of $5, $10 a day, just to give me some data to play with whilst I'm working most of my time on the main campaign. So I wouldn't be thinking about this as a target, you know, I must have 3% of my budget for testing because you know, if you've got $100, you're spending a day, do you really want to spend $3 only on testing? That's entirely your call. But I'd certainly say when you're starting off, your test budget is far higher as a percentage, but then it will start to dwindle. It will be miniscule as you start to hit $50 a month, $100 a month, $250 a month. That testing budget will be so, so tiny. So don't focus heavily on the percentages, but just kind of keep an eye out and make sure you are putting money to testing. The retargeting is a budget which I based on frequency. What I want to make sure is I'm hitting people as much as possible in retargeting, but not too much. I want to make sure that I'm getting the kind of Goldilocks idea of getting the kind of middle ground between keeping the customers happy with, you know, not over hitting them with creative, but also making sure you're maximizing your opportunity for the sale as well. So this I would be fluctuating the spend based on the feedback from increasing that frequency with more ads, maybe refreshing my creative, keeping an irrelevant score, just making sure my CPA comes in. So for example, the goal here has been $25 for this particular client, which is slightly off by a dollar. And really you can see here there's opportunity to maybe shift spend away from our traffic campaign into website conversion. Here our prospecting work isn't too far off our main target. We can do some optimizations here. And because these spends are so small, they have a minimal impact on CPA as well. So really what I would be doing is to try and balance my CPA as best as possible between getting as much budget into retargeting as possible, but not overreaching that as well.
